Unit 2 Project

2026-01-27

LECTURE #6

#Q1 Biscoe island has the most penguins. Adding theme_minimal gets rid of the gray background and makes the background white. It also changes the grid lines from white to gray.

ggplot(data=penguins,aes(x=island))+geom_bar()+theme_minimal()

#Q2 The distribution is bimodal. Between bins: bins=10 was a little too wide making it look more like a normal distribution, bins=30 showed the bimodal distribution and it was not too wide or too narrow, bins=60 was too narrow and made the distribution look very inconsistent.

ggplot(data=penguins, aes(x=body_mass_g))+geom_histogram(bins=10)
## Warning: Removed 2 rows containing non-finite outside the scale range
## (`stat_bin()`).

ggplot(data=penguins, aes(x=body_mass_g))+geom_histogram(bins=30)
## Warning: Removed 2 rows containing non-finite outside the scale range
## (`stat_bin()`).

ggplot(data=penguins, aes(x=body_mass_g))+geom_histogram(bins=60)
## Warning: Removed 2 rows containing non-finite outside the scale range
## (`stat_bin()`).

Adelie and Chinstrap have lower body masses and have a similar distribution with a high peak in the middle, while Gentoo has higher body masses and the distribution is a little wider. The approximate mean is not the same because Gentoo penguins have a much higher body mass than Adelie and Chinstrap, so Gentoo’s mean would be higher than theirs.

ggplot(data=penguins, aes(x=body_mass_g))+geom_histogram(bins=30)+facet_wrap(~species)
## Warning: Removed 2 rows containing non-finite outside the scale range
## (`stat_bin()`).

#Q3 Gentoo penguins have the longest flipper length.

ggplot(data = penguins, aes(x = species, y = flipper_length_mm))+geom_boxplot()
## Warning: Removed 2 rows containing non-finite outside the scale range
## (`stat_boxplot()`).

Yes males and females differ in body mass, males have a higher body mass than females.

ggplot(data = na.omit(penguins), aes(x = sex, y = body_mass_g,))+geom_boxplot()

Yes adding the (fill=species) shows each species body mass within the female and male categories. It also gives them each a different color so you can differentiate between the species. It shows that in both makes and females Gentoo have a higher body mass then Adelie and Chinstrap. Adelie and Chinstrap have body masses closer to each other in male and female categories.

ggplot(data = na.omit(penguins), aes(x = sex, y = body_mass_g,fill = species))+geom_boxplot()

#Q4 The relationships seems to be positive because as the x axis increases so does the y axis.

ggplot(data = penguins, aes(x = bill_length_mm, y = bill_depth_mm))+geom_point()
## Warning: Removed 2 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range
## (`geom_point()`).

My original finding was correct but it is a lot more clear seeing the chunks of species in different colors I can see that they are all increasing so they have a positive correlation,

ggplot(data = penguins, aes(x = bill_length_mm, y = bill_depth_mm, color=species))+geom_point()
## Warning: Removed 2 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range
## (`geom_point()`).

Yes the lines are a line of best fit so they fit the data pretty well showing the general positive trend for each species.

ggplot(data = penguins, aes(x = bill_length_mm, y = bill_depth_mm, color=species))+geom_point()+geom_smooth(method = "lm")
## `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'
## Warning: Removed 2 rows containing non-finite outside the scale range
## (`stat_smooth()`).
## Warning: Removed 2 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range
## (`geom_point()`).

#Q5 Gentoo shows the strongest relationships because the points are very close together and shows a positive correlation.

ggplot(data = penguins, aes(x = flipper_length_mm, y = body_mass_g)) + geom_point() + facet_wrap(~ species)
## Warning: Removed 2 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range
## (`geom_point()`).

Gentoos seem to have the highest body mass and flipper length among the three species in male and females. But in all three species the males have higher flipper length and body mass than females, because the male clusters are higher up in body mass and are clustered near the longer flipper length.

ggplot(data = penguins, aes(x = flipper_length_mm, y = body_mass_g)) + geom_point() + facet_grid(sex ~ species)
## Warning: Removed 2 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range
## (`geom_point()`).

#Q6

ggplot(penguins, aes(x = flipper_length_mm, y = body_mass_g))+geom_point(alpha = 0.7)+geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE) +facet_grid(sex ~ species) +labs(x = "Flipper Length (mm)",y = "Body Mass (g)")+theme_minimal()
## `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'
## Warning: Removed 2 rows containing non-finite outside the scale range
## (`stat_smooth()`).
## Warning: Removed 2 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range
## (`geom_point()`).

#Q7 It was really fun to get to make scatter plots, histograms, box plots, and bar graphs. I enjoyed seeing the data in a plot rather than just in a table as we did prior. It was a little difficult doing question 6 just because it had so many parts so I had to remmeber and learn how to do all the commands together.

LECTURE #7

penguins_messy <- read.csv("penguins_messy.csv")

#Q1 This checks each value in the species_messy column to see if “adelie” appears. It puts out a vector of true or false specifying which rows have “adelie” and which do not.

str_detect(penguins_messy$species_messy, "adelie")
##   [1]  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE
##  [13]  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE
##  [25]  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE
##  [37]  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE
##  [49]  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE
##  [61]  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE
##  [73]  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE
##  [85]  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE
##  [97]  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE
## [109]  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE
## [121]  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE
## [133]  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE
## [145]  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE
## [157] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE
## [169] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE
## [181] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE
## [193] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE
## [205] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE
## [217] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE
## [229] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE
## [241] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE
## [253] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE
## [265] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE
## [277] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE
## [289] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE
## [301] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE
## [313] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE
## [325] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE
## [337] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE

This removes all question mark characters from the sex_messy column by replacing them with an empty string.

str_replace_all(penguins_messy$sex_messy, "\\?", "")
##   [1] "M" "f" "f" NA  "f" "M" "f" "M" NA  NA  NA  NA  "f" "M" "M" "f" "f" "M"
##  [19] "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "M" "f" "M" "f" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M"
##  [37] "M" "f" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "M" NA  "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M"

This cleans up the data and makes it more compact getting rid of extra spaces.

str_squish(penguins_messy$island_messy)

#Q2 316 is the number of rows with valid ids.

penguins_messy <- penguins_messy %>%
  mutate(valid_id = str_detect(sample_id, "PAL-[0-9]{3}"))
valid_count <- penguins_messy %>% 
  pull(valid_id) %>% 
  sum()
print(valid_count)
## [1] 316

#Q3

penguins_messy <- penguins_messy %>%
  filter(valid_id == TRUE)

#Q4

penguins_messy <- penguins_messy %>%
  mutate(file_date = str_extract(file_name,
  "^[0-9]{4}-[0-9]{2}-[0-9]{2}"),
    file_id   = str_extract(file_name, "PAL-[0-9]{3}"))
penguins_messy %>%
  select(file_date, file_id) %>%
  head(10)
##     file_date file_id
## 1  2024-01-14 PAL-535
## 2  2024-01-17 PAL-442
## 3  2024-01-08 PAL-668
## 4  2024-01-26 PAL-920
## 5  2024-01-06 PAL-223
## 6  2024-01-08 PAL-899
## 7  2024-01-08 PAL-268
## 8  2024-01-19 PAL-139
## 9  2024-01-14 PAL-847
## 10 2024-01-24 PAL-238

#Q5

penguins_messy <- penguins_messy %>%
  mutate(island = str_squish(island_messy))
unique(penguins_messy$island)
## [1] "Torgersen" "BIS-COE"   "Dream"

#Q6

penguins_messy <- penguins_messy %>%
  mutate(sex = sex_messy %>%
      str_replace_all("\\?", "") %>%
      str_to_upper())
unique(penguins_messy$sex)
## [1] "M" "F" NA

#Q7

penguins_messy <- penguins_messy %>%
  mutate(bill_mm =
      str_replace_all(notes,"(.*bill=)|mm", "") %>%
      as.numeric())
## Warning: There was 1 warning in `mutate()`.
## ℹ In argument: `bill_mm = str_replace_all(notes, "(.*bill=)|mm", "") %>%
##   as.numeric()`.
## Caused by warning in `str_replace_all(notes, "(.*bill=)|mm", "") %>% as.numeric()`:
## ! NAs introduced by coercion
head(penguins_messy$bill_mm)
## [1] 39.1 39.5 40.3   NA 36.7 39.3

#Q8 It was very interesting to use the new stringr functions and see what they produce. It was a little confusing getting used to where and when to use the different functions. Also in question 4 the codes got kind of long and complicated with all the different numbers and parts so that was a little confusing. I feel like I have a general idea on how regular expressions are used. I just need a little more practice and getting used to them.

LECTURE #8

penguins <- palmerpenguins::penguins

#Q1 There 344 rows in the original and 333 rows in the cleaned.

penguins_clean <- penguins %>%
  drop_na(bill_length_mm, sex)
original_rows<- nrow(penguins)
cleaned_rows <- nrow(penguins_clean)
print(paste("Original number of rows:", original_rows))
## [1] "Original number of rows: 344"
print(paste("Cleaned number of rows:", cleaned_rows))
## [1] "Cleaned number of rows: 333"

#Q2 The total nuber of rows in penguins_dup is 363, after removing duplictaes it’s 333. I think duplicates can mess up summary statistics because it can skew the mean, median, and standard deviation because certain numbers are being repeated. In plots, it can mess up certain patterns like it might make certain bars taller or cause certain trends to look stronger because of the duplicate values.

penguins_dup <- penguins_clean %>%
  bind_rows(slice_sample(penguins_clean, n = 30, replace = TRUE))
nrow(penguins_dup)
## [1] 363
nrow(distinct(penguins_dup))
## [1] 333

#Q3 The resulting data frame has 8 columns.

penguins_selected <- penguins_clean %>%
  select(species, island, sex, where(is.numeric))
ncol(penguins_selected)
## [1] 8

#Q4

penguins_clean %>%
  select(starts_with("bill"))
## # A tibble: 333 × 2
##    bill_length_mm bill_depth_mm
##             <dbl>         <dbl>
##  1           39.1          18.7
##  2           39.5          17.4
##  3           40.3          18  
##  4           36.7          19.3
##  5           39.3          20.6
##  6           38.9          17.8
##  7           39.2          19.6
##  8           41.1          17.6
##  9           38.6          21.2
## 10           34.6          21.1
## # ℹ 323 more rows
penguins_clean %>%
  select(ends_with("_mm"))
## # A tibble: 333 × 3
##    bill_length_mm bill_depth_mm flipper_length_mm
##             <dbl>         <dbl>             <int>
##  1           39.1          18.7               181
##  2           39.5          17.4               186
##  3           40.3          18                 195
##  4           36.7          19.3               193
##  5           39.3          20.6               190
##  6           38.9          17.8               181
##  7           39.2          19.6               195
##  8           41.1          17.6               182
##  9           38.6          21.2               191
## 10           34.6          21.1               198
## # ℹ 323 more rows
penguins_clean %>%
  select(matches("^(bill|flipper)"))
## # A tibble: 333 × 3
##    bill_length_mm bill_depth_mm flipper_length_mm
##             <dbl>         <dbl>             <int>
##  1           39.1          18.7               181
##  2           39.5          17.4               186
##  3           40.3          18                 195
##  4           36.7          19.3               193
##  5           39.3          20.6               190
##  6           38.9          17.8               181
##  7           39.2          19.6               195
##  8           41.1          17.6               182
##  9           38.6          21.2               191
## 10           34.6          21.1               198
## # ℹ 323 more rows

#Q5 Using across(where(is.numeric)) is must more efficient and less likely to have errors than typing out each numeric column name where you could accidentally skip one or have a spelling error.

penguins_clean %>%
  group_by(species) %>%
  summarise(across(where(is.numeric), mean, na.rm = TRUE))
## Warning: There was 1 warning in `summarise()`.
## ℹ In argument: `across(where(is.numeric), mean, na.rm = TRUE)`.
## ℹ In group 1: `species = Adelie`.
## Caused by warning:
## ! The `...` argument of `across()` is deprecated as of dplyr 1.1.0.
## Supply arguments directly to `.fns` through an anonymous function instead.
## 
##   # Previously
##   across(a:b, mean, na.rm = TRUE)
## 
##   # Now
##   across(a:b, \(x) mean(x, na.rm = TRUE))
## # A tibble: 3 × 6
##   species   bill_length_mm bill_depth_mm flipper_length_mm body_mass_g  year
##   <fct>              <dbl>         <dbl>             <dbl>       <dbl> <dbl>
## 1 Adelie              38.8          18.3              190.       3706. 2008.
## 2 Chinstrap           48.8          18.4              196.       3733. 2008.
## 3 Gentoo              47.6          15.0              217.       5092. 2008.

#Q6

penguins_clean %>%
  mutate(
    bill_length = 
    case_when(
      bill_length_mm < 40 ~ "short",
      bill_length_mm < 50 ~ "medium",
      bill_length_mm >= 50 ~ "long")
  )
## # A tibble: 333 × 9
##    species island    bill_length_mm bill_depth_mm flipper_length_mm body_mass_g
##    <fct>   <fct>              <dbl>         <dbl>             <int>       <int>
##  1 Adelie  Torgersen           39.1          18.7               181        3750
##  2 Adelie  Torgersen           39.5          17.4               186        3800
##  3 Adelie  Torgersen           40.3          18                 195        3250
##  4 Adelie  Torgersen           36.7          19.3               193        3450
##  5 Adelie  Torgersen           39.3          20.6               190        3650
##  6 Adelie  Torgersen           38.9          17.8               181        3625
##  7 Adelie  Torgersen           39.2          19.6               195        4675
##  8 Adelie  Torgersen           41.1          17.6               182        3200
##  9 Adelie  Torgersen           38.6          21.2               191        3800
## 10 Adelie  Torgersen           34.6          21.1               198        4400
## # ℹ 323 more rows
## # ℹ 3 more variables: sex <fct>, year <int>, bill_length <chr>

#Q7 Penguins_clean has 8 columns and penguins_instrumented has 11.

instrument_meta <- tibble(
  instrument_id = c("caliper_A", "caliper_B", "caliper_C"),
  calibration_mm = c(0.2, 0.5, 1.0),
  manufacturer = c("Mitutoyo", "Fowler", "Generic")
  )
penguins_instrumented <- penguins_clean %>%
  drop_na(bill_length_mm) %>%
  mutate(
    instrument_id = case_when(
      year == 2007 ~ "caliper_A",
      year == 2008 ~ "caliper_B",
      year == 2009 ~ "caliper_C")) %>%
  left_join(instrument_meta, by = "instrument_id")
ncol(penguins_clean)
## [1] 8
ncol(penguins_instrumented)
## [1] 11

#Q8 The part of this lesson that was most interesting was using across(where(is.numeric)) in Q5 to compute the mean of all numeric columns. It was cool to find a new more efficient way to find all these values so we don’t have to type out all the numeric column names. The most confusing aspect was probably completing Q7 just because it was a very long code with a lot of different parts. I feel like these tips will help me with data analyses in the future especially the across(where(is.numeric)) function and the function for eliminating duplicates. I think these functions both will allow to compute things about the data set quickly, like mean, standard deviation, etc. as well as organizing the data sets.
